changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "Mother's Diary Ⅲ"

TextENGNew: One sunny parent observation day.
changeReason: 参観日 is a 'parent observation day' (parents observe classes), not a 'Parent-Teacher Day' (which implies conferences/meetings); these are distinct school events.

TextENGNew: During a cooking class, that child prepared curry rice with practiced, confident hands.
changeReason: The Japanese uses あの子 ('that child'), a distancing pronoun reflecting the mother's complicated feelings; the original's 'my child' conveys a warmer, possessive tone that misrepresents the narrator's emotional distance.

TextENGNew: While children her own age fumbled clumsily with their kitchen knives, her knife work was remarkably skilled.
The teacher came over and smiled, saying it must be the result of practicing at home.
I managed to deflect with a vague smile and a nod.
...Because I had never once taught that child how to make curry rice.
changeReason: Same as entry 14815: the Japanese uses あの子 ('that child'), not 'my child'; the distancing language is thematically important to the mother's alienation from Rika.

TextENGNew: And yet, with practiced ease, she peeled vegetables and added them to the pot in the correct order, starting with those that took longest to cook.
An ordinary parent might clap with surprise and pride at their child's unexpected accomplishment.
But in my case, it was different.
...This curry-making skill, too, must have been taught to her by someone behind my back.
changeReason: 預かり知れないところ means 'beyond my knowledge/behind my back,' not 'when I wasn't taking care of her'; the original misinterprets the phrase as a childcare gap rather than secrecy.

TextENGNew: ...Thinking that, I quietly fell into an unpleasant mood.
When I asked, it seemed she could also sew and do the laundry.
I had never taught her how to do those, and again, never saw her do it around the house.
Be it cooking or sewing or the laundry.
...Surely once again, without my knowledge, the elderly villagers had been filling that child's head with all sorts of things.
And not only that -- they were stuffing her full of dubious superstitions, molding her into this supposed reincarnation of Oyashiro-sama.
changeReason: 生まれ変わり means 'reincarnation' (rebirth of a soul), not 'living incarnation' (a god made flesh); these are theologically distinct concepts, and in Higurashi's context the villagers believe Rika is Oyashiro-sama reborn.

TextENGNew: I said as much to my husband, suggesting that we should keep our child away from the elderly folk.
But my husband, who was in the position of being the head priest of the Furude Shrine, had little power against the elderly who supported it.
...He took the rather uncommitted stance of saying that as long as she was being doted upon, wasn't it fine?
I argued against that.
That she was our child, and should be a completely normal girl.
That what the elderly were expecting, that she was the living incarnation of Oyashiro-sama, was simply odd.
The elderly villagers believe that child has divine powers.
They claim she can predict tomorrow's weather, but I've seen her leave without an umbrella and come home drenched on many occasions.
changeReason: Old misidentifies the subject: Japanese describes others claiming Rika predicts weather, not the mother herself.

TextENGNew: They say she has clairvoyant knowledge of faraway events, but that's nothing more than her parroting back the news she watches so avidly.
changeReason: Original omits the 'they say' framing (という) that attributes the clairvoyance claim to the villagers; additionally, 受け売り specifically means 'parroting/repeating what she heard' rather than the vaguer 'second-hand knowledge.'

TextENGNew: As for knowing things she shouldn't know -- that can be fully explained by the villagers who whisper things to her in secret and those who then go around proclaiming it as proof.
changeReason: The Japanese describes a two-part mechanism: 影で吹き込んだ人間 (people who secretly feed her information) and 吹聴する人間 (people who then publicize it as evidence); the new translation clearly articulates this deliberate conspiracy the mother perceives, while the original muddles it.

TextENGNew: Though... there certainly were days when everyone was sure it would be sunny, and yet that child stubbornly refused to go out without her umbrella.
It would occasionally rain, and in the end, we would be saved.
...There might also have been times when Rika knew about major foreign accidents before the news reported them.
I thought that she must have heard it break over the radio or something.
...Knowing things she shouldn't know -- wasn't an example of that right in front of me at this very moment?
That child was making curry rice without anyone having taught her.
No, no... that couldn't possibly be the case.
Somebody had taught her, indoctrinated her.
Somewhere beyond my sight, someone was filling Rika's head with things.
changeReason: Same issue as entry 14823: 預かり知れぬところ means 'beyond my knowledge/sight,' not 'when I wasn't taking care of her'; the original misinterprets the phrase as a childcare gap rather than secrecy.

TextENGNew: ``Furude-san's curry is absolutely splendid. I'll give it a gold star!``
``Where did you learn to cook, Furude-san? At home?``
``......Yes. At home.``
The other parents in attendance were impressed.
Lies. All lies.@k...I never taught her anything.
Who is it? Who?@k...Who has been filling my child with all of this?
She is not the reincarnation of Oyashiro-sama -- she is my ordinary daughter!
changeReason: Same as entry 14829: 生まれ変わり means 'reincarnation,' not 'living incarnation'; these are theologically distinct concepts relevant to the Higurashi narrative.
